speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two with Stephanie Heller. Stephanie, before the Germans came into Prague, were there any signs of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have known that some young people were learning, for instance, Spanish. They were trying to get visa to some country in-- outside of Europe. And we were not really taking it very seriously, because we didn't have the means to go anywhere, and we didn't have a chance to get a visa to other country. We heard that there is problem in Austria, because my mother's cousin came with his family when Hitler occupied-- annexed-- annexed Austria. They escaped to Prague, and they lived in our flat. And it was very cramped. They had three children. And we knew that they had been persecuted, even though that the cousin was Jewish, but his wife was not. And the children were Mischlings. That means mixed marriages. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '38. And they stay for short term with us. And then the children were safe. They were sent to Sweden, because some relation was there. The father stayed behind. The mother went back to Vienna. And she died. I don't-- didn't know about it then. And the father, we thought, was taken to Theresienstadt. He visited us occasionally when we were alone then in-- in a orphanage. But we knew that there is danger, because there was a rumor. And there were people from Poland. Somehow the rumors came. But we-- and the Germany, when there was the Kristallnacht, we-- night-- we have heard about it. But we all thought, oh, well. That is far away-- far away. We-- this wouldn't come to Czechoslovakia. We are a democratic republic. Nothing like that could happen here. But we, as young-- younger adults, didn't take really much of the hint. And our parents wouldn't discuss it with us. They were frightened. They were worried. But they didn't discuss it-- didn't discuss it with us. But we knew that the situation is very grave, because of the economic situation. The parents lost their jobs. My mother had to do some sewing at home to earn living. What we earned in the-- in the orphanage, we have supplied them with some help. I know that we were always sorry we can't do much more for them. But my father was hawking some soap maybe or, you know-- from house to house. Because he-- nobody would employ him as a Jew. We, as young girls, I remember that having this yellow Star of David, we walked once on the street, and we were caught by a Gestapo. And we were forced to go to clean floors in a villa in some better-- better part of the town, which was taken away from Jews. And German were taking over. And we had to clean the floors. We didn't know how to clean parquet floors, I know. I remember that one of the German soldiers even showed us how to do it. Because we didn't know how to clean floors and polish it. And we had to-- if in-- we-- we hadn't been not able to go not only to cinema and theaters. And parks were forbidden to us. We were not allowed to sit in the tram in the same carriage, as the first carriage. We had only the second carriage. On the-- there were some-- some streets which were not for us. We couldn't go to the center of the city shopping-- the-- shoppings. They are done at the different times for Jews. You must realize that during the war, when Hitler came, when the war started then, they were issued-- identification cards we had before-- but ration cards for textile.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you given the identification cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't remember when was that. But I know that we had identification cards. And we had on it stamped J. That's for Jude-- Jew. And our rations were also stamped with J. And our rations were different from the main population. And we had our shopping done at different times. And then when there was shortage of something, we had been not given, because first came the gentile population. We had some friends, gentile friends, who might have helped occasionally. But it was our policy not to involve them too much, because it was forbidden. And our mother was very fair lady. And she didn't want to harm anybody. And she had a lot of non-Jewish friends, neighbors, and so on. But she was very careful not to be seen with them very much, because she didn't want to bring them into danger. I remember that there was-- we didn't have wireless. It was taken away from us. And neighbor had a wireless. And there was always these reports. And we came secretly into the flat of the people to listen and-- when we visited our mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the whole, the life was always uncertain. We didn't know what the next day will bring. There was not-- I wouldn't say that at that time it was fear. The uncertain way of life made it very miserable. We never knew what rules will come the next day. One-- yes? I was going to ask-- how did you learn of the new laws or rules?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't recollect how we did learn about them. It was in-- when we were in the orphanage. Then we were told by the management, this and that is not to be done, and that there were written probably some-- on the-- somewhere on the street was written that Jews are not allowed to do this or that. I really can't recollect those. You know, I have been trying to forget lots from my-- of-- from my past. And that is very difficult to get it back from the hidden little places in the mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How long were you in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have been there from, I think, '41. I must tell you that while we were in the orphanage, our parents were called up, and they were forcibly removed from the flat. They had to take only small belongings, small suitcase. And they have been assembled in a exhibition building where all other Jews were assembled who were chosen to be sent to Poland, to Litzmannstadt, which forced the first transport. My parents and my little sister were in the first transport when they took Jews away from Prague to East. They were told they are going to work in East. Germany needs their labor. And my sister and I, we hurried. We were told that they are going. They probably phoned the orphanage. And we were hurrying there. And we came with them when they were going to the exhibition building to assemble. And we were going with them. And we wanted to come with them voluntarily. And we were not allowed.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were standing outside of the gate. And we were crying. And some SS men came to us and ask us, what is the matter? And we said, we would like to go with our parents. And we are not allowed to enter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, go home and get your stuff. Get your suitcase, and come next day in the morning. You can go with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were very, very happy, because we didn't want to be separated from them. We were 18 then, 17. And we came to the orphanage. We didn't have much. We didn't have suitcases. But we got quickly what we could together.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, we came to the meeting point. Nobody was there anymore. They were sent away in the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we haven't seen them ever since. I remember the last moment when they were sent-- taken in.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was the last glimpse we had of them. We haven't seen them anywhere. That was the last time. [CRYING] Well, we were not allowed to-- we were not allowed to go with them. So we had to go back. And we lived in the orphanage. We were working. We were sending parcels to the parents too, because we have got a address where they are, a-- a card, a postcard. And we were trying to get together whatever we could. I don't think that they ever got it. And there was even a special post office. We were not allowed to go to a ordinary post office. We had to travel to the other car-- side of the town at certain time to send the parcel from the certain post office. And sometimes there was a big queue. And we couldn't even get in. That was dreadful. And we had to go next day. But on the whole, we have been young. And we have taken it in our stride. And we have hoped that one day we might be sent after them, and we will somehow see them again. And while we were with the young people, the young children, the orphans in the-- in the orphanage, were being sent one by one away. Because there was established later Theresienstadt ghetto, which was later concentration camp called. But the ghetto was in Czechoslovakia. And kids were sent there. And we have been helping them to pack their little rucksacks and baked challah to give them some food to take with them. And later, we might have sent-- we might have met those kids in Theresienstadt when we were sent there ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the selection made?

speaker: interviewee
text: The selections were made-- I don't know how were they made. They must have been made by certain systems. I don't know. For us, it was everything just a mystery. And we had to obey. We did-- if somebody didn't obey, he had to bare the consequences-- being sent away, straight away, or being kicked out and sent to Gestapo. And we were at the Gestapo to register for-- I don't know exactly what was it. And we were very frightened. Because Gestapo was always-- not only the name, but to how they looked, how they behaved-- was very frightening. But going back to the being left alone-- when the parents were sent out of the flat, they have-- the flat was sealed. And our-- whatever we had-- our possessions left in the flat, we couldn't take. We couldn't go in anymore. But we were living in the orphanage. So we have had our stuff. So-- so we really didn't miss everything. While we were in the orphanage, we-- there was-- Sudetenland was occupied. And Jews came from Sudentenland to Prague. And one young man came to work in the orphanage too. He was a student of engineering. And because of student of engineering, he has been given a job, a extra job, to work on washing machines, automatic-- not automatic-- big washing machines in the-- industrial washing machine in the orphanage. And that was my first husband. We met, there. We didn't work together, because we have been in the orphanage itself with the children, and so on. But he was working in the Waschraum, it was called. And we met. We fell in love. And his friends-- his friend-- he had a friend. And he-- she was sent also to Litzmannstadt with the same transport as my parents were. And that started our friendship. And then we fell in love. And we didn't want to be separated. So if we got married. We were-- I was only 18 then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Egon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Egon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Egon, E-G-O-N, Kunwalder, K-U-N-W-A-L-D-E-R-- Kunwalder. And I was not able to get married without a permission of parents. But at that time, my uncle was living still in Prague. So he gave the permission that I can marry. I was not of age. And Egon's parents, they also were living in Prague. And we were married at a registry. The problem was that it was not a real marriage-- not as such, as you would think now. I didn't have a white dress. I didn't have flowers. I had a black dress-- black suit-- which was made out of a dinner suit of somebody-- and no flowers and no photographer. My sister even wasn't allowed to come to the wedding, because she has been working. And it was no fuss wedding. And-- but I was married. And we were not separated afterwards. We were staying together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you actually live in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- we-- we have been given a room in a-- of a apartment of Jewish people who had to take tenants, because Jews were not allowed to live in an apartment on their own. So there, one room of their apartment was given to us. And we lived there. There was even a maid of those people who was very kind to us. She taught me how to do something, how to make a cake or something, because I wasn't really taught to cook. But it was not for a long time. It was-- we were married in March. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '42. And I was very sad, because my parents didn't know that I'm married. And his parents were sent to Theresienstadt soon after we were married. And so I didn't have really mother-in-law for long. And they were-- were not able to be with us anyway. We visited them once or twice. The thing is that in May '42, my sister who was single at that time-- I don't know what the kind of system was taking people into transport was-- told that she has to go to Theresienstadt ghetto. And of course, I didn't want to be separated from her. So Egon and I, we volunteered to come just as well. And we were allowed to assemble in the exhibition building. We were taken in. We were registered as for the transport. And after one night spent on the floors there and being taken-- even our wedding ring was taken away from us-- my husband's hair was shorn-- or cut short. And the next day, in the morning, Egon and I, we have been told we are not allowed to accompany my sister on the transport, because are saboteurs. We have been working under the Germans in the Jewish orphanage. And we are saboteuring our jobs. And we are not allowed to go. We are Jews. We can't just choose whatever to do. We have our masters to listen to. So we have to be-- we had to leave Annetta there and go back to the orphanage and work there, and wait for our turn. Fortunately, Annetta, when she left for Theresienstadt, was able to smuggle through some Czech police, gendarme, some note that we knew that she's in Theresienstadt. Because transports were going through Theresienstadt, and they were going away from there. And we didn't know. We knew that they are going to east-- people from there. But we didn't know where. And we didn't know their fate. We didn't know much about Theresienstadt at all. We just were told that Theresienstadt is a place where the Jews will be all living together happy. And they will work. But we didn't know much about it. My husband and I got a call in December, in '42, to assemble. And we were sent to Theresienstadt. And while Annetta and I were separated, she has made friends in Theresienstadt. She started to work as a nursing aide in a children's hospital at the infectious disease. And young, she made friends. And she made friends with Ghettowache. Jewish people have been the guardians of the ghetto. The establishment was led-- led by-- the administration was by Jewish elders in Theresienstadt. And the policing was done by Jewish people-- Jewish Ghettowache-- ghetto police. And Annetta made friends with one of them and told him, when the transports come from Prague, my sister might can just as well. And she looks like me. And you-- you watch out for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was like that. When I came, somebody came to me, a Ghettowache, and said, you have a sister Annetta? And I said, yes. He said, I know where she is, and I will bring her over. So he-- we were united again. And because of Annetta, we have been always inseparable. Because of her, I got the same position as her. And we were together.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband has been given a job as a Ghettowache. He was good looking, tall, strong. And he couldn't choose not to. He didn't like it. They had a uniform-- kind of cap and a band on their arm. He didn't like to be Ghettowache, because it meant to push people in queues and to be orderly, you know, to keep order. And that was not up to his liking, because he saw the misery. And he was a very pessimistic, young man. And he told me on occasions that we could meet. We didn't live together. In Theresienstadt, I and Annetta, we lived in the hospital. We had a better accommodation than the other people in ghetto, because it was overcrowded. And it was very difficult to find a place, and so on. But as working, we have been, in the hospital, we have been accommodated with other nurses. And my husband was accommodated in some barracks on a bunks as a Ghettowache. We could meet occasionally. But we didn't live together. And he was very pessimistic. And he told me, you're not getting out of it. He has seen the misery better than I, because I have been with Annetta, separated from the others, working in the infectious disease. We were not allowed to mingle with the others very much, not to spread diseases, because Germans were very careful not to spread diseases. They were killing us. They were killing people, sending them to gas chambers later. But they were very careful not to have epidemics in the ghetto. It was a bureaucracy. And we have been quite content to work in the hospital, because we were learning how to look after the children. We were happy to do something for the children. We were still able to give them medications, and change their beddings, and wash them, and feed them, and so on. Maybe that's-- life in Theresienstadt gave us then the feeling that we wanted to something for later in our life-- to do something like that too. Annetta has met Egon's parents, my parents-in-law, in the ghetto. And she met also our uncle and auntie. They were people who are very used to much better life, much better way of-- the better-- better quality of life. My father-in-law was a engineer. And he was used to being waited on. And his wife was always looking after him very well. He used to walk in the ghetto with the white gloves. And when my sister has seen them, she was very upset, because she knew that are deteriorated. They didn't have enough to eat. Old people were given only portions. The other people, the younger people who were working, get-- probably, got probably better rations. My uncle and auntie were elderly. And Annetta has met them there too. And she was very, very upset to see them. I didn't meet them anymore. But she told us about it. They were sent to-- from Theresienstadt-- we don't know where, really. They might have been sent to Auschwitz. We don't know. They didn't come back. And I wouldn't have expected them to come back, because they've been very, very depressed, I would say. And when I saw the life later, and I saw the old people in Theresienstadt too, I have seen what depression means. People didn't really care if they really live or not. But young people in Ghetto were having quite chances to lead a culture life. There were underground theaters. There were singing. There were people, children. We were not children anymore. We were adults. But youngsters were taken to-- not every youngster had the chance-- but from Prague, many of them were put to separate houses. And they had tutors who were teaching them. They had good education. They have been active in the place, and so on. They have been working on the field in the Theresienstadt, on the-- they were taken sometimes out to work-- some were young people-- out of Theresienstadt to work. We were not. I remember only one time. That must have been some special-- special chance that we had. We have been taken out of Theresienstadt to work on fields to gather the hay. And it was wonderful. It was beautiful there. We have had freedom in the field. As it was, we have been locked in the-- in the Theresienstadt. I don't know if I am jumping too much. Maybe you want to ask questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's fine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the ghetto-- Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what sort of sanitary provisions were made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for the people who were crowded in the barracks, they were old. They were Kaserne-- old barracks for the soldiers-- which were occupied by the Jews. The Christian people who used to live in that town were sent elsewhere. And their houses were taken by-- all by SS or by-- by the guards. Some of the administration people had better-- better quality of housing. But on the whole, it was very difficult for people to manage overcrowding. And sanitation was there. But it was slowly deteriorating because of the overcrowding. We, in the hospital, had still chances to have to keep ourselves clean. And you know, we haven't been really in the-- we have been in the one hospital, which was a Kaserne. I don't know-- don't know what is the Kaserne called-- the place for soldiers to live. One building, big building, which was a hospital, and we were-- have been-- have been living there. And we had chance to keep ourselves clean. But the ghetto was not easy. And there was always the transport coming in and out. People were brought into Theresienstadt from Holland, from France, from Germany. And it was only, I would say, a station. I would call it like a exchange station. Some were brought in. And some were brought out. The level of the inhabitants had to be always kept on certain level. I don't know how many people were there. And a lot of people were sent away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any contact with the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some contact. People were originally-- there were people getting parcels. For instance, friends-- the husband was Jewish; the wife was not. And she was sending him parcels. She got contact with Czech-- the Czech police and was able to even get some messages, and so on. But slowly, it was not being brought through. The parcels were not always coming-- not to us. There was-- our ghetto was-- I must mention that. That was a model ghetto. Theresienstadt was a model ghetto, because there was an international Red Cross sent over to see how the Jews are looked after, how they are living there. And there was streets cleaned up, painted houses, shop opened. All the clothes that they were taken from Jews on arrival were in the shops. There was money printed-- ghetto money-- which, of course, was no value at all. But it was used at the visit of the Red Cross that people went and did some shopping. There was-- and that used to money. There was cafeteria on the street. There was orchestra playing, like in Vienna in the-- you know, on the promenade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? Or when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been '43. I'm not sure. I haven't been out at that time, because we were in the hospital. I didn't take part. Now, I'm not sure which-- what time was it. But it really bluffed everybody, because children were out there playing, given some sweets or something. People were given parcel at the post office. But on the other side, when they went out, the parcel was taken away from them. People were forced to act. They were forced to act. I don't know if they have done it with pleasure or not, if they believed that it will be some change done. Maybe they have done it on purpose to show that it could be done, that they could live there well. I don't know. The people who participated just did it. And it bluffed the whole world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Stephanie.
